retrieved_docs.title: Acute Inflammatory Response -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Acute inflammation initiates following a specific injury, triggering the release of soluble mediators such as cytokines, acute phase proteins, and chemokines. These substances promote the migration of neutrophils and macrophages to the inflammation site, representing a crucial component of the innate immune response during acute inflammation. [4] If the acute inflammation does not quickly resolve, it progresses to subacute inflammation. If inflammation persists beyond 6 weeks, it transitions from subacute to chronic, marked by the migration of T lymphocytes and plasma cells to the site of inflammation. Prolonged inflammation without recovery leads to tissue damage and fibrosis. [3] Additional varieties of cells, such as macrophages and monocytes, also contribute to acute and chronic inflammation. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Inflammation -- Introduction -- Acute Inflammation
retrieved_docs.content: Tissue damage due to trauma, microbial invasion, or noxious compounds can induce acute inflammation. It starts rapidly, becomes severe in a short time and symptoms may last for a few days for example cellulitis or acute pneumonia. Subacute inflammation is the period between acute and chronic inflammation and may last 2 to 6 weeks.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Inflammatory Response -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammation is an essential aspect of the innate defense mechanism of the body against infectious or noninfectious etiologies. This mechanism is nonspecific and immediate. [1] The 5 fundamental signs of inflammation include heat, redness, swelling, pain, and loss of function. Increased blood flow leads to redness and heat, while swelling results from fluid accumulation. Pain arises from releasing stimulating chemicals, and loss of function reflects a combination of factors. These signs are evident in acute surface inflammation but may not all be observable in internal acute inflammation, particularly within internal organs. [2] Inflammation can be categorized into 3 types based on the duration of the response to the injurious cause—acute inflammation, which manifests immediately after injury and typically lasts for a few days; chronic inflammation, which can persist for months or even years if acute inflammation fails to resolve; and subacute inflammation, a transitional phase from acute to chronic lasting from 2 to 6 weeks. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Pathology, Inflammation -- Issues of Concern -- Acute or Chronic Inflammation
retrieved_docs.content: The acute inflammatory process is a 4-stage process that begins with the initiation phase, triggered by the injury. This phase involves changes to the microcirculation, leading to fluid loss and the migration of white blood cells to the injured area. The amplification phase follows, where chemical substances direct additional types of white blood cells to the site, enhancing the response. The rapid neutralization of the injury and debris removal characterizes the destruction phase. Finally, the termination phase requires the action of chemical substances to halt or regulate the inflammatory process, preventing excessive damage if unchecked. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Inflammatory Response -- Clinical Pathology
retrieved_docs.content: The assessment of inflammatory markers is used to detect acute inflammation, potentially indicating a specific disease and assessing treatment response. The most common inflammatory markers are C-reactive protein (CRP), erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR or Sed-rate), and procalcitonin (PCT). [4] CRP is an acute-phase reactant widely used in laboratory testing to assess inflammation. [10] CRP is synthesized in the liver, and its transcription is directly regulated by interleukin-6 (IL-6) and indirectly influenced by IL-1β, tumor necrosis factor α (TNF-α), and other cytokines. Research has indicated its ability to bind to bacteria and tissue membranes, enhancing phagocytosis. In addition, CRP can activate the classical complement pathway. CRP is usually present in plasma below 5 mg/L. [11] Various stimuli, such as infection, major trauma, surgery, and chronic inflammatory conditions, can significantly increase CRP production. Following these stimuli, CRP concentrations rise within hours, doubling within 5 to 8 hours. In addition, it is widely acknowledged that mild inflammation and viral infections cause CRP concentrations to elevate within the range of 10 to 40 mg/L. In contrast, active inflammation and bacterial infection result in 40 to 200 mg/L concentrations. Concentrations exceeding 200 mg/L are typically observed in severe bacterial infections and burns. CRP has a half-life of 19 hours. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Inflammation -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammation is part of the body's defense mechanism. It is the process by which the immune system recognizes and removes harmful and foreign stimuli and begins the healing process. Inflammation can be either acute or chronic. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Inflammatory Response -- Causes
retrieved_docs.content: The causes or inducers of inflammation can be classified into 2  main groups—exogenous and endogenous inducers. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Pathology, Inflammation -- Issues of Concern -- Acute or Chronic Inflammation
retrieved_docs.content: Cryotherapy is an effective treatment for acute inflammation caused by musculoskeletal injury, leading to decreased pain and more rapid  return to participation.  [8] The primary mechanism is reduced sensory nerve transmission at the site, whereas peripheral vasoconstriction slows the metabolic rate. Reducing oxidative stress reduces secondary ischemic injury and alleviates inflammation. [9] Cryotherapy is most effective when applied soon after the injury, within 24 to 48 hours. [10]  Chronic inflammation has a slow onset of days, a long duration of years, less prominent classical signs and symptoms, and cellular infiltrate composed of monocytes, macrophages, and lymphocytes. [11] Chronic inflammation can arise from persistent infections caused by microorganisms that are difficult to eliminate, such as mycobacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites, triggering delayed-type hypersensitivity reactions that may lead to granulomatous responses or unresolved acute inflammation evolving into a chronic state. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Inflammatory Response -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Acute inflammation is an immediate, adaptive response with limited specificity caused by noxious stimuli, such as infection and tissue damage. This beneficial response protects against infectious organisms, including Mycobacterium tuberculosis , protozoa, fungi, and other parasites. However, it can become detrimental if not regulated, such as seen in septic shock. [6] The inflammatory pathway involves inducers, sensors, mediators, and effectors. [7] Inducers initiate the inflammatory process, including infectious organisms or noninfectious stimuli such as foreign bodies and signals from damaged tissues or necrotic cells. These inducers activate sensors, which are specialized molecules that stimulate the mediators. Mediators are endogenous chemicals that can induce pain, regulate the inflammatory process, and promote tissue repair. They also activate the effectors, the tissues, and the cells involved in the response. [8] These components work together, leading to various inflammatory pathways depending on the stimuli type. The ultimate goal of the inflammatory process is to restore homeostasis, regardless of the cause. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Pathology, Inflammation -- Issues of Concern -- Acute or Chronic Inflammation
retrieved_docs.content: Acute inflammation has a rapid onset of minutes or hours, typically resolves within a few days, has classic signs and symptoms, and has a cellular infiltrate primarily consisting of neutrophils. [4] The erythema observed in acute inflammation results from increased blood flow to the affected area due to vasodilation. Vasodilation is triggered by various mediators, with histamine as a notable example, acting on vascular smooth muscle. This process initially affects the arterioles and opens new capillary beds in the affected area. [5] Besides blood vessels, lymphatic vessels are active in acute inflammation. In inflammation, lymph flow is increased and helps drain edema fluid that accumulates because of increased vascular permeability. Along with fluid, leukocytes, cell debris, and microbes may also enter the lymph. [6] Similar to blood vessels, lymphatic vessels proliferate during inflammatory reactions to handle the increased load. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Acute Phase Reactants -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: APPs are non-specific markers of inflammation, and the tests used should be interpreted in conjunction with history, physical examination, and other laboratory tests and imaging. Their levels will be elevated during both acute and chronic inflammation. However, the highest levels are attained in acute inflammation during an acute infection or after trauma resulting in CRP of 50 to 100 mg/L and ESR exceeding 50 mm/hour.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Inflammation -- Introduction -- Chronic Inflammation
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammation is also referred to as slow, long-term inflammation lasting for prolonged periods of several months to years. Generally, the extent and effects of chronic inflammation vary with the cause of the injury and the ability of the body to repair and overcome the damage. This article reviews chronic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Inflammatory Response -- Biochemical and Genetic Pathology
retrieved_docs.content: The complement system is the fourth group of mediators capable of activating acute inflammation. Complements are a set of proteins that interact with each other to initiate a cascade. Many complements can be activated through various pathways, such as the classical, alternative, or mannose-binding lectin pathways. Among the most important complements in acute inflammation are C3a and C5a, which mediate the anaphylatoxins. C5a is known for its chemotactic effect on neutrophils, while C3b is an opsonin for phagocytosis. These complements can further activate the membrane attack complex (MAC), activating neutrophils, monocytes, and mast cells. [23] [24] The last mediator is the Hageman factor—a component of clotting factors crucial in inflammation. Activation of this factor subsequently triggers the kinin system and the production of bradykinin. Bradykinin, in turn, enhances the permeability of blood vessel walls, allowing for leakage. This leakage results in swelling, a component of acute inflammation. [25] [26] Other mediators and biomarkers of acute inflammation include reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen oxide species (RNOS), cytokines (such as IL-6, TNF-α, and chemokines), the formation of DNA adducts, acute-phase proteins such as CRP, inflammation-related growth factors, transcription factors (such as NF-kB), and major immune cell types. The specific mediators and immune cells involved vary and depend on several factors, such as the type of inducer, the duration of the injury, and multiple genetic loci. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Inflammation -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Recurrent episodes of acute inflammation. However, in some cases, chronic inflammation is an independent response and not a sequel to acute inflammation for example diseases such as tuberculosis and rheumatoid arthritis.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Acute Phase Reactants -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Macrophages sense bacterial products through Toll-like receptors (TLR) and regulate the inflammatory response. TLR4 senses the gram-negative bacterial lipopolysaccharide (LPS). Downstream signaling leads to the activation of the transcription factor, nuclear factor kappa beta (NF-kB), and increased production of IL-6. IL-6 activates liver production of other acute-phase proteins. Tissue injury and trauma can also activate the acute-phase reaction (APR). The purpose of acute-phase proteins released during APR is to participate in blood coagulation, defense against infection, transport metabolites, nutrients and hormones, and maintenance of homeostasis. IL-6, IL-1beta, TNF-alpha, glucocorticoids, and growth factors are the primary mediators of acute-phase proteins (APP) gene expression. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Inflammatory Response -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2104
retrieved_docs.content: Acute bronchitis is classified as a subtype of الْتِهابِ شعبي and is clinically associated with الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ. The condition الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ serves as a parent category for التهاب رئوي وليدي, indicating that التهاب رئوي وليدي is a specific form of pulmonary inflammation. This hierarchical relationship highlights that acute bronchitis is linked to broader pulmonary inflammatory processes, and that الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ encompasses more specialized entities such as التهاب رئوي وليدي, which together inform a comprehensive understanding of respiratory inflammatory disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 245
retrieved_docs.content: while linking acute glomerular inflammation to broader kidney disease categories and acute renal failure, providing a comprehensive framework for interoperable clinical data and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 153
retrieved_docs.content: روماتزمي، غير ذلك وغير محدد. Chronicity also characterizes secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease, and chronic inflammatory small bowel disease, indicating that prolonged inflammation or metabolic dysfunction underlies persistent pathology in the cardiovascular, renal, hepatic, endocrine, and gastrointestinal domains. Uncontrolled disease states are reflected in the General clinical state and are closely tied to diabetes mellitus with complications. Together, these relationships highlight how acute rheumatic inflammation, functional failure, chronic disease progression, specific anatomic sites, and multilingual terminology interconnect to inform comprehensive patient evaluation, monitoring, and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1856
retrieved_docs.content: الاعتماد على الكحول (alcohol dependence) is clinically associated with اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد (liver inflammation), indicating that chronic alcohol use can contribute to inflammatory damage of the liver. Hepatitis is classified as اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد, meaning that hepatitis represents a specific type of liver inflammation often caused by viral infection, autoimmune processes, or toxic insults. Together, these relationships highlight that liver inflammation can arise both from alcohol dependence and from hepatitis, underscoring the importance of evaluating alcohol use and identifying hepatitis when assessing patients with hepatic inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 894
retrieved_docs.content: is identified as a member of الفشل الكلوي الحاد (SMQ), linking inflammatory kidney disease to the same acute kidney failure classification used for hemodialysis‑related conditions. Together, these interconnections highlight how hemodialysis‑related disorders, their sudden‑onset presentations, associated comorbidities, and classification within acute kidney failure frameworks are tightly integrated in medical understanding and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2603
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney inflammation (التهاب الكلية الحاد) is classified as a member of the Acute Renal Failure Standardized MedDRA Query (الفشل الكلوي الحاد (SMQ)), indicating that this condition is included within the broader safety‑monitoring group for acute renal failure. Recognizing acute kidney inflammation as part of this SMQ helps clinicians and pharmacovigilance professionals identify and assess renal adverse events more comprehensively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 501
retrieved_docs.content: Meningitis, an inflammation of the protective membranes covering the brain and spinal cord, encompasses several specific forms, including bacterial meningitis (التهاب السحايا الجرثومي), which represents a distinct subtype caused by bacterial infection. Recognizing bacterial meningitis as a particular category within the broader condition of meningitis is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted antimicrobial therapy, and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a severe, necrotizing form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis), representing a specific subtype within the broader category of glomerular inflammation. This disease is closely associated with متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome), a clinical entity in which acute lung involvement—often pulmonary hemorrhage—occurs together with rapid deterioration of renal function. Understanding that necrotizing glomerulonephritis both belongs to the spectrum of glomerulonephritis and can manifest as part of a pulmonary‑renal syndrome is essential for timely diagnosis, comprehensive evaluation of both kidney and lung pathology, and the implementation of appropriate immunosuppressive and supportive therapies to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 713
retrieved_docs.content: The category “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions, including فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (aplastic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (hemolytic anemia not caused by autoimmunity), التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). These entities are grouped together because they involve immune system dysregulation, whether through autoantibody‑mediated attacks, cell‑mediated immune injury, or broader immune‑related inflammatory processes. While most of the listed disorders are driven by autoimmune mechanisms, the non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia is included in the classification for organizational purposes despite its different pathogenesis. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying immune component helps clinicians identify common diagnostic pathways and consider immunomodulatory therapies across different organ systems, thereby improving patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 659
retrieved_docs.content: network of vascular and cardiac pathologies that share anatomical sites, inflammatory mechanisms, temporal sequences, and clinical comorbidities, informing comprehensive cardiovascular assessment and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 370
retrieved_docs.content: nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome” group is shown to have acute glomerulonephritis as a parent condition, underscoring the progression from acute inflammatory injury to chronic nephrotic states. Overall, these interconnected classifications emphasize that CKD resides within a network of renal pathologies ranging from acute glomerular inflammation to systemic immune‑mediated diseases such as lupus nephritis and thrombotic microangiopathies like hemolytic uremic syndrome. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in understanding disease progression, overlapping diagnostic categories, and the importance of integrated management across acute and chronic kidney disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2835
retrieved_docs.content: Other and ill-defined heart disease, which is synonymous with تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب, denotes a category of cardiovascular conditions that are not precisely classified. This category includes the SMQ اعتِلاَل عَضَل القَلب, indicating that myocardial inflammation is considered a member of other and ill-defined heart disease. Recognizing that other and ill-defined heart disease and تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب refer to the same clinical grouping helps clinicians organize and address these ambiguous cardiac disorders within patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1626
retrieved_docs.content: Acute bronchitis is identified as a specific form of الِتهابِ شعبي, indicating that it falls under this broader category of inflammation. As an acute condition, acute bronchitis involves a short‑term inflammation of the bronchial tubes, leading to symptoms such as cough, sputum production, and chest discomfort. Recognizing acute bronchitis as a subtype of الِتهابِ شعبي helps clinicians classify the disease within a larger framework of respiratory inflammatory disorders, facilitating appropriate diagnosis, treatment, and patient education.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 193
retrieved_docs.content: is identified as a severe form of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (glomerular disease) linked to داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑basement membrane disease) and classified within the broader categories of nephritis, nephrosis, and renal sclerosis; it is associated with post‑infectious glomerular inflammation (التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي) and contributes to اليوريمية and kidney dysfunction. The drug‑interaction SMQ is also a member of the nephritis/nephrosis/renal‑sclerosis group, while acute kidney inflammation (التهاب الكلية الحاد) belongs to the الفشل الكلوي الحاد SMQ, highlighting how overlapping glomerular injury, systemic drug‑reaction syndromes, and acute inflammatory processes collectively drive renal dysfunction and failure, necessitating integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.
